active_paradigms.idea: Replace Richards exponent with a Gompertz (double-exponential) sigmoid template that captures asymmetric amyloid lag phase
active_paradigms.description: Amyloid ThT curves have an asymmetric S-shape: a long flat lag, sharp acceleration, then plateau approach. The Gompertz function y = plateau*exp(-exp(-rate*(time - lag))) + baseline naturally produces this asymmetry with the SAME number of constants as a plain logistic and is numerically bulletproof: the inner exp argument is bounded, exp(-exp(.)) always lies in (0,1). Build expression as plateau*sp.exp(-sp.exp(-rate*(time-halftime))) + baseline, where rate = c0*monomer**c1 (concentration power-law preserved) and halftime = c2*monomer**c3 - c6*monomer*0 ... keep halftime = c2 + c5*x2 + c3*monomer (use affine seed/monomer terms to avoid power singularities). Use constant_symbols(7): c0 rate-scale, c1 rate-power, c2 lag-intercept, c3 lag-monomer-slope, c5 lag-seed-slope, plateau c4, baseline c6. Initialize rate-scale 1.0, power 0.0, lag 0.5, slopes 0.0, plateau 1.0, baseline 0.0. This removes the redundant Richards exponent (which overlapped with rate) and replaces it with a fundamentally asymmetric kernel that better matches the nucleation-elongation lag. Single evaluate_expression call.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: mean validation NMSE across datasets via better lag-phase asymmetry fit; keep complexity low (~one fewer exp-power than Richards) to preserve parsimony factor
active_paradigms.cautions: Keep the inner rate*(time-halftime) bounded by not over-scaling c0; start rate-power c1 at 0 so initial fit is a stable Gompertz. Avoid monomer**c3 in halftime if c3 can go negative with small monomer — prefer affine c3*monomer. Ensure exactly one evaluate_expression and straight-line code with from __future__ import annotations.

active_paradigms.idea: Use a Hill-type saturating fraction-converted template with concentration-modulated cooperativity exponent
active_paradigms.description: Model normalized aggregation as a saturating power law in time: y = plateau * t^n / (k + t^n) + baseline, the Hill equation, which is the canonical fraction-converted form for cooperative nucleation-polymerization and gives a sigmoid in linear-time when n>1. Implement growth = time**n / (c2 + time**n) with n = c1 (cooperativity, init ~3) and half-saturation c2 = c3*monomer**c4 - c6*seed made affine-safe. To keep time**n real and finite for time in [0,1] including time=0, add a tiny offset: use (time)**n where n>0 stays finite at 0 (0**n=0), but guard n via using sp.exp(c1) so the exponent is always strictly positive (n=exp(c1)>0), preventing negative/zero exponents that cause singularities at time=0. Set half-saturation strictly positive similarly with c2_pos = sp.exp(c2) to keep denominator > 0. expression = c5 * growth + c7, growth = time**sp.exp(c1) / (sp.exp(c2)*monomer**0 + time**sp.exp(c1)) ... allow concentration to enter half-saturation: denom_const = sp.exp(c2) * (1 + c3*monomer + c6*seed) kept positive by the exp dominating. Use constant_symbols(6-7). This is a structurally distinct rational/power family rather than an exponential-sigmoid, targeting datasets where the logistic tail decays too fast or the lag is power-law rather than exponential.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: mean validation NMSE on curves with power-law lag or slow saturating tails that the logistic mismatches; maintain numerical safety with exp-wrapped positive exponents and denominators
active_paradigms.cautions: time**sp.exp(c1) at time=0 must be 0 not NaN — ensure exponent strictly positive via sp.exp. Keep denominator strictly positive so no division blowup. Watch that the affine concentration multiplier (1 + c3*monomer + c6*seed) does not go negative; if risk is high, drop seed term. Only one evaluate_expression call, no conditionals.

active_paradigms.idea: Stretched-exponential (Kohlrausch/Avrami) approach-to-plateau template for nucleation-growth kinetics
active_paradigms.description: The Johnson-Mehl-Avrami-Kolmogorov (JMAK) law for nucleation-and-growth gives fraction converted y = plateau*(1 - exp(-(rate*time)^beta)) + baseline, a stretched exponential that produces a lag (when beta>1) and a smooth saturating plateau, distinct from logistic/Gompertz. Implement: arg = (c0*monomer**c1*(time)), with stretch exponent beta = sp.exp(c2) kept strictly positive so (arg)**beta is real only when arg>=0 — guarantee arg>=0 by squaring the inner term or using sp.exp form: instead use growth = 1 - sp.exp(-sp.exp(c2)*(c0**2)*monomer**c1*time**c3) where time**c3 with c3=exp(...) positive keeps everything nonnegative and finite (time in [0,1], so time**positive in [0,1]; the whole exponent argument is >=0 so 1-exp(-.) lies in [0,1]). Add affine seed acceleration: multiply the rate by (1 + c5*seed) but bound via using c0**2 and exp so the rate stays positive. expression = c4*growth + c6. Use constant_symbols(7): c0 rate, c1 monomer-power, c2 stretch (via exp), c3 time-power (via exp), c5 seed-accel, c4 plateau, c6 baseline. Initialize so beta=1, time-power=1 → pure exponential-rise, then optimizer adds curvature. This targets the plateau-approach shape and lag simultaneously with strictly nonnegative, always-finite arithmetic.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: mean validation NMSE especially on cross-protein evaluation-only datasets where the saturating approach and lag must generalize; robust because the exponent argument is provably nonnegative
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure the entire exponent argument is nonnegative by using positive-only factors (c0**2, sp.exp wrappers, time**positive); never take a fractional power of a possibly-negative base. The seed multiplier (1 + c5*seed) could go negative — clamp by using sp.exp(c5*seed) instead so it is always positive. Initialize stretch and time-power exponents to 1 (c2=c3=0). Exactly one evaluate_expression call, straight-line code only.
